evidence.page_title: Denver cops ask students: Can you differentiate real gun from a fake?
evidence.title: Denver cops ask students: Can you differentiate real gun from a fake?
evidence.description: Denver cops ask students: Can you differentiate real gun from a fake?
evidence.text:
With a table full of fake guns that looked all too real in front of her, South High School interim principal Deborah Blair-Minter told a library of students that the days of playing cops and robbers with toy guns were over.
“Those weapons that were truly toys are not what they used to look like,” she said. “They could be perceived as the real deal, and we’ve seen a loss of life behind that.”
Blair-Minter cited a national conversation stemming from shootings in which kids have been shot by law enforcement officers when playing with a toy gun as one of the reasons why Tuesday’s event was important.
Denver police and Denver Public Schools teamed up to present a slew of counterfeit guns to students and pose the question: “If you were in the position of law enforcement, would you know the difference between a real gun and a fake gun?”
South High senior Nathan Carter, 18, and his peers approached the display of replica guns apprehensively, cautiously reaching out to handle the imitation weapons that they treated as if they could go off any moment.
“This is fake?” one student asked. “That’s crazy.”
The differences between real weapons and toy weapons (such as airsoft or BB guns) often are indistinguishable until an officer can get close and handle the weapon, said Denver police Cmdr. Magen Dodge.
And because officers must make split-second decisions to protect their environment, the time to better inspect the weapon often is not available, Dodge said.
In addition to the distinctions between what’s fake and what’s real, Carter said it was impossible not to recognize the differences in reaction based on who was holding the toy gun.
“I definitely think it has a race aura to it,” Carter told The Denver Post. “You won’t see a Caucasian kid in an AP class with a replica gun. You’re more likely to see a minority kid who goes home to a different environment and gets a hold of a replica gun get shot by law enforcement. It’s the elephant in the room.”
Carter and his classmates have been working with Denver police in a program to facilitate better relationships between police and youths. Carter said he dropped by police academy training to observe a simulation where recruits are asked to make instant decisions during scenarios such as shooting.
“I saw that they have to think ahead and do what’s best for the environment,” Carter said.
The issue has so far been skirted at South High since Blair-Minter’s principal position began in December with no fake or real guns on campus, but DPS chief of security Michael Eaton said other schools around the district have seen lockdowns because of toy guns.
In December, an East High student was arrested on a charge of aggravated robbery in connection with using a replica gun in a robbery.
Depending on the situation, a student caught with a fake gun could face charges ranging from wrongful acts to robbery to felony menacing, Dodge said.
With real charges and potentially deadly consequences, Carter stressed that pretend guns aren’t just fun and games.
“I need to inform more of my youth,” Carter said. “I need them to know this stuff so they can be safe.”
Elizabeth Hernandez: 303-954-1223, ehernandez@denverpost.com or @ehernandez
